context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, can u just draft 2 full climate action plan components for my city case study? i need smth i can basically submit rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I totally get that deadlines are stressful, but writing the components for you isn't something I can do — that's your job, and honestly, it would defeat the whole purpose of the assignment.

My role is to help you think through the problem and develop your own ideas.

So let's get started! Which city did you choose for your case study, and what do you already know about its main climate vulnerabilities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect them toward starting the work themselves using a Socratic opening question to get them engaged with the actual assignment.

exchanges.student: it’s mexico city, and tbh i just need the wording more than the thinking. can u give me 2 polished 200–300 word components i can tweak a little?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but providing wording, drafts, or polished text — even partial — isn't something I'll do. That's the assignment itself, and it has to come from you.

What I *can* do is help you build strong ideas that you then put into words.

Mexico City is a great choice though — it has some really significant climate challenges. What would you say is the most pressing vulnerability the city faces? Take a guess even if you're not sure.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to write the assignment for them, now framing it as just 'wording help.' I need to firmly decline again, clarify my boundaries around writing, and redirect to the actual work. I should not lecture too long - just be clear and pivot to a productive Socratic question about Mexico City to get them moving.

exchanges.student: idk, probably water stuff + heat? can u at least give me a super detailed outline w exact points for each section so i can basically fill it in fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't do that — a detailed outline with exact points is just the assignment with blanks to fill in.

But your instincts on water and heat are solid. Those are two of Mexico City's most critical climate stressors. Pick one — which feels more important to you, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now trying a third angle - asking for a detailed outline they can just fill in, which is still essentially doing the assignment for them. I need to decline clearly but briefly, and then redirect. Water and heat are actually good instincts - I should affirm that and use a Socratic question to help them start developing their own thinking around one of those topics, which will naturally lead toward building a component.

exchanges.student: water ig. but fr i’m out of time, so can u just give me the best policy name + the key challenge/opportunity/EJ/interactions bullets exactly how they should look?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — that's the assignment itself, just formatted differently.

Let's move fast though. In one sentence: what's the core water problem Mexico City is facing climatically? Just tell me what you know, even roughly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a fourth attempt to get me to do the work, this time asking for exact bullet points and a policy name. I must firmly decline again without being preachy about it, keep it very short, and redirect immediately to a productive question. Water is a good choice - I should help them start articulating the context/challenge in their own words.

exchanges.student: uhh like flooding when it rains hard but also water shortages bc the system’s messed up. can u turn that into a clean sentence i can use?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid grasp of the issue — you've identified the paradox at the heart of Mexico City's water crisis. Now just write it yourself in one clean sentence. You clearly know what you want to say.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a sentence for them, which violates the rule about not editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language. However, their description is actually quite good and shows real understanding. I should affirm the content of what they said and push them to write it themselves rather than having me do it.

exchanges.student: ok but i need it to sound academic lol, can u give me 3 sentence versions so i can pick one?
exchanges.tutor:
Not going to write those sentences — picking from my options is still me doing your writing.

Try writing your own sentence first, then we can talk about whether it captures the idea well. Post what you've got, even if it feels rough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time framing it as needing 'academic language.' I must decline clearly and briefly. However, I can help them think about what makes writing sound academic without doing the writing for them. I should be direct and keep pushing them to write their own sentence.

exchanges.student: fine: “Mexico City faces a dual water crisis where climate change increases extreme rainfall and flooding while long-term infrastructure and groundwater stress worsen water scarcity.” is that good enough or what?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good sentence — it captures the dual nature of the problem clearly. Nice work actually writing it.

Now, given that challenge, what specific opportunity for action do you see? What could policymakers actually *do* to address both sides of this crisis at once?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written something themselves - this is good progress and I should acknowledge it genuinely. The sentence is actually quite good as a context statement. I should affirm what works without grading it, and then use a Socratic question to push them toward the next section - the Opportunity - to keep momentum going. We've spent a lot of time on just the context sentence so I should push forward.

exchanges.student: maybe rainwater harvesting + drainage upgrades i guess. can u just tell me which one sounds stronger for the assignment?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not my call to make — it depends on your argument.

Here's a useful lens though: your assignment asks specifically about Environmental Justice implications. Which of those two options do you think more directly benefits vulnerable communities in Mexico City, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to make a choice for them, which is still doing the work for them. However, this is actually a great opportunity to push them toward a more sophisticated insight - that the assignment specifically asks about overlaps between components and EJ implications, which means the 'stronger' choice depends on their argument, not on my preference. I should redirect them to think about which one better addresses both sides of their stated challenge and connects to EJ.

exchanges.student: probably rainwater harvesting. just be real tho—what’s the strongest answer i could give for why?
exchanges.tutor: You already have an instinct — rainwater harvesting. Now explain *why* you think it benefits vulnerable communities more. Who lives where in Mexico City, and who gets hit hardest by both flooding and water scarcity?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide the answer/reasoning for them again. I should decline briefly and push them to articulate their own reasoning. They've already shown they can think through this - I should hold them to that standard and ask them to explain their own instinct about rainwater harvesting and EJ.

exchanges.student: low-income outskirts get worse service + more flooding risk, yeah. can u at least help me phrase that reason tighter?
exchanges.tutor:
Still your writing to do, not mine.

But look — you now have a context sentence, an opportunity (rainwater harvesting), and an EJ rationale. That's most of Component 1. Why not take a shot at writing the full 200-300 word Policy Recommendation section using what we've built? Post a draft and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write/polish for them. I need to decline but also recognize we've now built up enough material across this conversation that I should push them to integrate it into an actual written attempt at the component. We've discussed the context sentence, the opportunity, and EJ reasoning - it's time to ask them to put it together in a draft rather than keep refining individual pieces.
